# Introduction

This article is related to a Linux Kernel 0-day I have recently found and exploited initially for Pwn2own 2026 (Red Hat category). Unfortunately, I waited too long to register (even if it was before the deadline) and ended up being ignored (legitimately due to the unexpected number of submissions) and so, I was not able to participate. The bug ended up being reported from another researcher, but no public details (and no CVE) have been still published about it and on its exploitability. The bug is just another bug in the network scheduler subsystem, the red scheduler. I have picked this subsystem as a target for three main reasons: unprivileged user namespaces are enabled in Red Hat, schedulers are an historically interesting subsystem (due to the high complexity and interactions with multiple network parts) and I had a fuzzer suitable for that kind of targets (syzkaller has its own limitations with this type of subsystems). Besides of the bug, the most interesting part is the exploitation strategy, turning an initially limited slab UAF into a page UAF and ultimately into a physical memory read and write.

# The bug

The vulnerability affects the "red network scheduler" (what a coincidence since I was targeting Red Hat) and has been introduced in commit 3f14b37 (January 2024) and fixed in commit a8a0289 (June 2026), lasting for 2.5 years. The fix is really straightforward and immediate:

--- a/net/sched/cls_api.c
+++ b/net/sched/cls_api.c
@@ -4049,6 +4049,9 @@ struct sk_buff *tcf_qevent_handle(struct tcf_qevent *qe, struct Qdisc *sch, stru
        skb_do_redirect(skb);
        *ret = __NET_XMIT_STOLEN;
        return NULL;
+   case TC_ACT_CONSUMED:
+       *ret = __NET_XMIT_STOLEN;
+       return NULL;
    }

    return skb;

I couldn't explain any better than what has been already done in the commit, so I'm just gonna extract the key part from it and add more details on top:

tcf_classify() can return TC_ACT_CONSUMED while the skb is held by the
defragmentation engine (e.g. act_ct on out-of-order fragments). When
that happens the skb is no longer owned by the caller and must not be
touched again.

tcf_qevent_handle() did not handle TC_ACT_CONSUMED: it fell through the
switch and returned the skb to the caller as if classification had
passed.

To see it in practice through the code, the red enqueue function (red_enqueue) is responsible to enqueue packets on its own scheduler. 

static int red_enqueue(struct sk_buff *skb, struct Qdisc *sch,
               struct sk_buff **to_free)
{
    switch (red_action(&q->parms, &q->vars, q->vars.qavg)) { // [2]
    case RED_DONT_MARK:
        break;

    case RED_PROB_MARK:
        // ..
        if (INET_ECN_set_ce(skb)) {
            q->stats.prob_mark++;
            skb = tcf_qevent_handle(&q->qe_mark, sch, skb, to_free, &ret); // [1]
            if (!skb)
                return NET_XMIT_CN | ret;
            // ..
        /* Non-ECT packet in ECN nodrop mode: queue it. */
        break;
    case RED_HARD_MARK:
        // ..
        if (INET_ECN_set_ce(skb)) {
            q->stats.forced_mark++;
            skb = tcf_qevent_handle(&q->qe_mark, sch, skb, to_free, &ret); // [1]
            if (!skb)
                return NET_XMIT_CN | ret;
        // ..
    }
    // ..
    ret = qdisc_enqueue(skb, child, to_free); // [5]
    // ..
}

The tcf_qevent_handle [1] function is called in multiple locations when red_action [2] returns RED_PROB_MARK or RED_HARD_MARK (basically when an user-controllable threshold is reached). When one of the two values is returned, and ECN is set (configurable from the tc command line), tcf_qevent_handle is called:

struct sk_buff *tcf_qevent_handle(struct tcf_qevent *qe, struct Qdisc *sch, struct sk_buff *skb,
                  struct sk_buff **to_free, int *ret)
{
    // ..
    switch (tcf_classify(skb, NULL, fl, &cl_res, false)) { // [3]
    case TC_ACT_SHOT:
        qdisc_qstats_drop(sch);
        __qdisc_drop(skb, to_free);
        *ret = __NET_XMIT_BYPASS;
        return NULL;
    case TC_ACT_STOLEN:
    case TC_ACT_QUEUED:
    case TC_ACT_TRAP:
        __qdisc_drop(skb, to_free);
        *ret = __NET_XMIT_STOLEN;
        return NULL;
    case TC_ACT_REDIRECT:
        skb_do_redirect(skb);
        *ret = __NET_XMIT_STOLEN;
        return NULL;
    }

    return skb;
}

// called from tcf_classify after few dereferences
TC_INDIRECT_SCOPE int tcf_ct_act(struct sk_buff *skb, const struct tc_action *a,
                 struct tcf_result *res)
{
    // ..
    err = tcf_ct_handle_fragments(net, skb, family, p->zone, &defrag);
    if (err)
        goto out_frag;

    // ..
out_frag:
    if (err != -EINPROGRESS)
        tcf_action_inc_drop_qstats(&c->common);
    return TC_ACT_CONSUMED; // [4]
}

This is where the bug lives, and is simply a missing case for the TC_ACT_CONSUMED value that can be returned from tcf_classify [3]. This value can be returned in multiple user controllable scenarios (e.g. when a fragmented packet is sent) and basically means that the ownership is transferred to the skb defragmentation engine, and the caller has to drop it. tcf_ct_act is called (after few dereferences) from tcf_classify and returns TC_ACT_CONSUMED [4] when tcf_ct_handle_fragments returns something, that is the case for a first fragmented packet sent. However, the switch condition in tcf_qevent_handle ignores the TC_ACT_CONSUMED case and fallthrough into returning the skb instead of NULL , ultimately ending up into enqueueing the packet in the scheduler queue [5] with qdisc_enqueue, indirectly calling bfifo_enqueue that will store the skb pointer (still not freed) as the last element of its queue [6] using the skb->next list.

static int bfifo_enqueue(struct sk_buff *skb, struct Qdisc *sch,
             struct sk_buff **to_free)
{
    if (likely(sch->qstats.backlog + qdisc_pkt_len(skb) <=
           READ_ONCE(sch->limit)))
        return qdisc_enqueue_tail(skb, sch);

    return qdisc_drop(skb, sch, to_free);
}

static inline int qdisc_enqueue_tail(struct sk_buff *skb, struct Qdisc *sch)
{
    __qdisc_enqueue_tail(skb, &sch->q);
    qdisc_qstats_backlog_inc(sch, skb);
    return NET_XMIT_SUCCESS;
}

static inline void __qdisc_enqueue_tail(struct sk_buff *skb,
                    struct qdisc_skb_head *qh)
{
    struct sk_buff *last = qh->tail;

    if (last) {
        skb->next = NULL;
        last->next = skb; // [6]
        qh->tail = skb;
    } else {
        qh->tail = skb;
        qh->head = skb;
    }
    qh->qlen++;
}

The skb pointer here is still not freed, but it will be just when the second fragmented packet arrives, leaving the dangling pointer as the last element of the queue. To be more precise, it will be freed in inet_frag_reasm_prepare with a consume_skb call (always starting from the tcf_classify call).

# The primitives

A first primitive might be already spotted from the shown code:

static inline void __qdisc_enqueue_tail(struct sk_buff *skb,
                    struct qdisc_skb_head *qh)
{
    struct sk_buff *last = qh->tail; // [7]

    if (last) {
        skb->next = NULL;
        last->next = skb; // [6]
        qh->tail = skb;   // [8]
    } else {
        qh->tail = skb;
        qh->head = skb;
    }
    qh->qlen++;
}

As we said, when the skb is erroneously inserted in the queue, it is still allocated but the ownership has been transferred. When the second fragment arrives, the skb is freed leaving a dangling pointer in the tail of the queue (qh->tail [7]), that is stored in the last variable and will lead to the primitive at [8] : a pointer write.

In the qh->tail = skb line [8], qh->tail is a dangling pointer and, most importantly, we can make skb a dangling pointer too by using the same technique as before. And here there is an interesting detail from an exploitation point of view. When sending two fragmented packets, the first one's length is set to 0, while the second one carries the summed size of both packets. Why does this matter? Because, if you closely look at the if condition in bfifo_enqueue, packets are only enqueued if they do not exceed the scheduler limit (adjustable from user-space), meaning that we can deterministically place a pointer that we can then free with same bug primitive (e.g. the first fragments of the two), leading to a more interesting freed pointer write at offset 0 (next offset from the sk_buff struct).

This is not the only primitive this bug offers. Another pretty powerful primitive come from the red_dequeue function (responsible to dequeue enqueued packets) and will be mentioned in the dedicated "Some notes on AI exploit development and the other primitive" chapter, with the reasons that led me choose this one instead.

# The exploit

# First assessment

At this point I had a pretty limited UAF primitive and one and an half week until the pwn2own registration deadline, I had to make it quick. I spent some time in finding the most interesting object I could reallocate with a Cross Cache, overwrite the first 8 bytes, and exploit its side effects, wether another pointer, a size value (considering the big 0xffff... semi controlled value), a refcount or something similar. The only Cross Cache constraint is that the dangling pointer starts from an order 1 (2 pages) cache skbuff_head_cache (in systems with 4 cores or more).

# First stage: from slab UAF to page UAF

At some point I remembered about a public exploit that was targeting exactly sk_buff, and this is where I stumbled into the pgv struct, even if it was not using it like I would.

static int
packet_setsockopt(struct socket *sock, int level, int optname, sockptr_t optval,
          unsigned int optlen)
{
    // ..
    switch (optname) {
    case PACKET_RX_RING:
    case PACKET_TX_RING: // [9]
    {
        // ..
        case TPACKET_V3:
        // ..
        if (!ret)
            ret = packet_set_ring(sk, &req_u, 0,
                          optname == PACKET_TX_RING);
        release_sock(sk);
        return ret;
    }
    // ..
}

static int packet_set_ring(struct sock *sk, union tpacket_req_u *req_u,
        int closing, int tx_ring)
{
    // ..
    if (req->tp_block_nr) {
        // ..
        order = get_order(req->tp_block_size);
        pg_vec = alloc_pg_vec(req, order);
        // ..
    }
    // ..
}

tatic struct pgv *alloc_pg_vec(struct tpacket_req *req, int order)
{
    unsigned int block_nr = req->tp_block_nr;
    struct pgv *pg_vec;
    int i;

    pg_vec = kcalloc(block_nr, sizeof(struct pgv), GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_NOWARN); // [10]
    // ..

    for (i = 0; i < block_nr; i++) {
        pg_vec[i].buffer = alloc_one_pg_vec_page(order); // [11]
        if (unlikely(!pg_vec[i].buffer))
            goto out_free_pgvec;
    }
out:
    return pg_vec;
    // ..
}

static char *alloc_one_pg_vec_page(unsigned long order)
{
    // ..
    buffer = (char *) __get_free_pages(gfp_flags, order); // [12]
    // ..
}

The pgv struct is incredibly interesting for its flexibility, but most importantly for its content. It can be allocated with a setsockopt syscall with PACKET_TX_RING [9] parameter, and it will allocate a user-controllable size object into an arbitrary generic cache (e.g. kmalloc-XXXX) given the user-controllable size specified in block_nr [10]. This allocation will be filled with page pointers [11] (allocated from __get_free_pages [12]) with the order value controllable from the user too. Since we can overwrite a pointer at offset 0, what's better than a list of page pointers?

Before diving into the reallocation of the second object, let's see what primitives gives us the corruption of one of the pgv pointers with a dangling pointer.

At first, I went down a rabbit hole that costed me an entire day and a bit more. It is possible, given an allocated pgv, to map it through the packet_mmap file operation.

static int packet_mmap(struct file *file, struct socket *sock,
        struct vm_area_struct *vma)
{
    // ..
    for (rb = &po->rx_ring; rb <= &po->tx_ring; rb++) {
        // ..

        for (i = 0; i < rb->pg_vec_len; i++) {
            void *kaddr = rb->pg_vec[i].buffer;
            // ..

            for (pg_num = 0; pg_num < rb->pg_vec_pages; pg_num++) {
                page = pgv_to_page(kaddr);
                err = vm_insert_page(vma, start, page); // [13]
                // ..
            }
        }
    }
    // ..
}

static int insert_page(struct vm_area_struct *vma, unsigned long addr,
            struct page *page, pgprot_t prot)
{
    // ..
    retval = validate_page_before_insert(vma, page);
    if (retval)
        goto out;
    retval = -ENOMEM;
    pte = get_locked_pte(vma->vm_mm, addr, &ptl);
    if (!pte)
        goto out;
    retval = insert_page_into_pte_locked(vma, pte, addr, page, prot);
    pte_unmap_unlock(pte, ptl);
out:
    return retval;
}
static int validate_page_before_insert(struct vm_area_struct *vma,
                       struct page *page)
{
    struct folio *folio = page_folio(page);
    // ..
    if (folio_test_anon(folio) || folio_test_slab(folio) ||
        page_has_type(page))
        return -EINVAL;
    flush_dcache_folio(folio);
    return 0;
}

As can be seen from the code, by calling mmap on that file descriptor, the mmap handler iterates over the just allocated pgv pointers and calls vm_insert_page [13] on each of them. Considering that we "control" one pointer, it might means that we can map to user-space an arbitrary pointer. And that's true, but not the entire truth.

First, pgv_to_page calls virt_to_page and does a big favor to us by page aligning the pointer (considering that we have replaced a page aligned pointer with a slab pointer that might not be page aligned) and then calls vm_insert_page on it. At first, the idea was trying to map some arbitrary memory to user-space (e.g. an entire slab page, a read-only memory or file, ..) but this is not permitted due to sanity checks in validate_page_before_insert that validates that the pointer is not part of a slab and is not anonymous (e.g. is not file backed). I still think that something interesting can be done with this primitive, but it was taking too much time, the deadline was approaching and I had to make it somehow.

Looking at other parts of the pgv code, I found another interesting primitive in the freeing process of the pgv pointers. While closing the socket file descriptor associated with the allocated pgv object, free_pg_vec iterates over each pointer and calls free_pages on each of them, permitting us to free our second dangling pointer again, but now with free_pages. This upgrades our primitive to a more powerful page-level UAF. And, similarly to the previous case, the pointer is page aligned directly inside the called free function.

However, the time spent on the mmap path did not ended up as an entire waste of time, even if it was not chosen as the final primitive. It has been used in the exploit to verify which pgv allocation was containing the second dangling pointer instead of a legitimate pointer by mapping it with mmap and verifying if it succeed or not. If the mmap call fails, it means that vm_insert_page has failed and we obtain two useful information: the first stage has been successful and we know precisely which allocation has been corrupted!

# Second stage: from page UAF to physical memory r/w

Once the first dangling pointer is cross cached into a pgv object (I deliberately avoid details about cross cache, spraying and similar since these are well known techniques), we trigger the bug a second time to store the second dangling pointer at offset zero of the first cross cached dangling pointer (the pgv object). We then proceed to drain the skbuff_head_cache in order to move the second dangling pointer to the order 1 page per-cpu freelist, with the objective to exploit the just created page-level UAF.

At this point I was trying multiple sprays to reclaim the page pointer with something interesting, and while I was spraying some anonymous regions, I found the holy grail from the gdb dump:

0x8000000114d1c067 0x8000000114d1c077
0x8000000114d1c087 0x8000000114d1c097

Look familiars? these are PTEs!

Side note: you might be wondering how I was able to reclaim an order 1 allocation with an order-0 PTE allocation with just some "random" spray. Well, that's because at first I was writing the exploit in a 2 core qemu environment where 256-bytes slabs are order-0, the case of skbuff_head_cache. However, on systems with 4 or more cores, these slabs are order-1 and the exploit was targeting that configuration.

I was immediately caught from that memory content, corrupting PTEs is an amazing primitive to ultimately achieve physical memory read and write.

However, as mentioned, I was first writing the exploit against a qemu environment with 2 cores, making the skbuff_head_cache an order-0 cache instead of an order-1 (the pwn2own setup could be likely with more than 4 cores). This made me restructure the page reclamation process with some more shaping. At first, the second dangling pointer is drained from the skbuff_head_cache to the order-1 page per-cpu freelist. Then, in order to reallocate it as an order-0 allocation, the pointer must leave the page per-cpu freelist and be inserted into the order-1 buddy freelist, permitting it to be splitted into two order 0 allocations (hence reclaimed as a PTE). I have accomplished that by using multiple pgv allocations to spray and shape the page freelists. Then, PTEs have been sprayed through anonymous memory using mmap, and the order-1 allocation has been successfully reclaimed from two order-0 allocations.

# Third stage: arbitrary physical memory read and write

Once the second dangling pointer is reclaimed as a PTE, we can trigger the free_page on it and reallocate with something more controllable from user-space. The msg_msgseg object seemed a reasonable choice due to its high-level of control on content and lifetime from user-space (except for the first next pointer that will be NULL for order-0 allocations but that will not be a problem), even better than msg_msg. Another key aspect of this target object is that it will be allocated into a kmalloc- cache, making it easily readable and writable from user-space (considering the usercopy mitigation).

At first, messages are sprayed with a leaked physical address (converted as PTE) from /sys/kernel/vmcoreinfo (readable from unprivileged users on all major distributions), to start with a valid physical address. To find the sprayed map that corresponds to the ovewritten PTEs, it is just necessary to verify the content of each mapped memory. Once the userland mapping has been found, in order to write new PTEs for the arbitrary physical read and write, we can receive and send all sprayed messages (msg_msgseg objects) one by one, to avoid the slab reallocation of different objects or even cache. During this process, it is also possible to find the correctly reallocated messages to optimize further PTEs modifications and have more stability.

# Fourth stage: uid=0

At this point, it was two days before the registration deadline, I was really excited to have made it and I just needed the lasts, easier steps. I sent the registration e-mail and took a day off, with the idea of coming back once I got the registration and enjoy the engineering process of transforming the physical memory read and write to root. However, I didn't received any response the next day, neither in the deadline day, and neither later. Since I had a lot of other work to do, I just finished with this primitive.

However, going from that really powerful primitive to root is just a matter of playing with it a bit. My first ideas were to scan memory starting from the vmcoreinfo leak (a dynamically allocated object, not part of any static kernel address), find a valid kernel address to break physical address randomization (enabled in Red Hat), and then overwrite the task_struct of the current process starting from the init_struct, or something similar. Unfortunately, I did not have the pleasure of doing that due to other pending projects with higher priority.

# Some notes on AI exploit development and the other primitive

During the exploitation process of this bug, since it could be considered a short-living bug given the pwn2own participation, I gave a try on using a public frontier model to write a full exploit: Opus 4.8 with Max Pro plan using Claude Code. I was surprised in both good and bad senses.

The good part is that it effectively wrote a working exploit, using an alternative primitive from the red_dequeue code that permits to redirect execution and achieve a ROP chain from the sk_buff destructor. It was using a side channel for KASLR leak and pwn2own rules consider using it as a partial win, so I wanted something more self-contained with the same bug. I run multiple sessions with qemu and debugging capabilities given to the agent, but it was not able to find a valid KASLR leak from that primitive.

The bad part instead is that it was not able to assist me much during the exploitation that I have explained in this article. I tried to redirect its thoughts with my ideas, giving some hints and some new paths, but after hours of background working, it was continuously falling back to the side-channel leak and ROP. At some point I was also doubting that my ideas would even work considering the continuous gaslighting from the model, but I had a sense that this bug could be more powerful and self-contained than what the model was saying. And, I was right.

That said, this is just my experience, these model are extremely powerful and are continuously improving, as public articles show in the exploit development field, and a lot depends on how you use them, how many tokens and so many other factors.

Also, no AI has been involved in writing this article. I found it pretty boring to find AI patterns in content mainly written for humans, that I prefer to see an imperfect, but personal and human recognizable, style of writing. If you see some errors, just ping me on X like it used to.

# Conclusions

I have not talked too much about spraying details, stabilization and reliability improvements, even if they were roughly 60-70% of the work. I wanted to focus more on general ideas of the exploitation strategy and bug root cause. I don't have plans to publish the exploit soon since the vulnerability is still pretty fresh, and there is also no CVE assigned at the moment of writing.

About the pwn2own failed registration, I was not the only one with that experience, and I don't blame the organization. It was unfortunate and I was really excited to participate on it, considering that I have also posted about a failed attempt few years ago with Hacktive Security. However, AI has changed a lot the cat and mouse game of defensive/offensive, and it will take some time (maybe few years) to find a new balance and more calm waters. Maybe, or maybe not.

When that happens the skb is no longer owned by the caller and must not be touched again. tcf_qevent_handle() did not handle TC_ACT_CONSUMED: it fell through the switch and returned the skb to the caller as if classification had passed. To see it in practice through the code, the red enqueue function (red_enqueue) is responsible to enqueue packets on its own scheduler. static int red_enqueue(struct sk_buff *skb, struct Qdisc *sch, struct sk_buff **to_free) { switch (red_action(&q->parms, &q->vars, q->vars.qavg)) { // [2] case RED_DONT_MARK: break; case RED_PROB_MARK: // .. if (INET_ECN_set_ce(skb)) { q->stats.prob_mark++; skb = tcf_qevent_handle(&q->qe_mark, sch, skb, to_free, &ret); // [1] if (!skb) return NET_XMIT_CN | ret; // .. /* Non-ECT packet in ECN nodrop mode: queue it. */ break; case RED_HARD_MARK: // .. if (INET_ECN_set_ce(skb)) { q->stats.forced_mark++; skb = tcf_qevent_handle(&q->qe_mark, sch, skb, to_free, &ret); // [1] if (!skb) return NET_XMIT_CN | ret; // .. } // .. ret = qdisc_enqueue(skb, child, to_free); // [5] // .. } The tcf_qevent_handle [1] function is called in multiple locations when red_action [2] returns RED_PROB_MARK or RED_HARD_MARK (basically when an user-controllable threshold is reached). When one of the two values is returned, and ECN is set (configurable from the tc command line), tcf_qevent_handle is called: struct sk_buff *tcf_qevent_handle(struct tcf_qevent *qe, struct Qdisc *sch, struct sk_buff *skb, struct sk_buff **to_free, int *ret) { // .. switch (tcf_classify(skb, NULL, fl, &cl_res, false)) { // [3] case TC_ACT_SHOT: qdisc_qstats_drop(sch); __qdisc_drop(skb, to_free); *ret = __NET_XMIT_BYPASS; return NULL; case TC_ACT_STOLEN: case TC_ACT_QUEUED: case TC_ACT_TRAP: __qdisc_drop(skb, to_free); *ret = __NET_XMIT_STOLEN; return NULL; case TC_ACT_REDIRECT: skb_do_redirect(skb); *ret = __NET_XMIT_STOLEN; return NULL; } return skb; } // called from tcf_classify after few dereferences TC_INDIRECT_SCOPE int tcf_ct_act(struct sk_buff *skb, const struct tc_action *a, struct tcf_result *res) { // .. err = tcf_ct_handle_fragments(net, skb, family, p->zone, &defrag); if (err) goto out_frag; // .. out_frag: if (err != -EINPROGRESS) tcf_action_inc_drop_qstats(&c->common); return TC_ACT_CONSUMED; // [4] } This is where the bug lives, and is simply a missing case for the TC_ACT_CONSUMED value that can be returned from tcf_classify [3]. This value can be returned in multiple user controllable scenarios (e.g. when a fragmented packet is sent) and basically means that the ownership is transferred to the skb defragmentation engine, and the caller has to drop it. tcf_ct_act is called (after few dereferences) from tcf_classify and returns TC_ACT_CONSUMED [4] when tcf_ct_handle_fragments returns something, that is the case for a first fragmented packet sent. However, the switch condition in tcf_qevent_handle ignores the TC_ACT_CONSUMED case and fallthrough into returning the skb instead of NULL , ultimately ending up into enqueueing the packet in the scheduler queue [5] with qdisc_enqueue, indirectly calling bfifo_enqueue that will store the skb pointer (still not freed) as the last element of its queue [6] using the skb->next list. static int bfifo_enqueue(struct sk_buff *skb, struct Qdisc *sch, struct sk_buff **to_free) { if (likely(sch->qstats.backlog + qdisc_pkt_len(skb) <= READ_ONCE(sch->limit))) return qdisc_enqueue_tail(skb, sch); return qdisc_drop(skb, sch, to_free); } static inline int qdisc_enqueue_tail(struct sk_buff *skb, struct Qdisc *sch) { __qdisc_enqueue_tail(skb, &sch->q); qdisc_qstats_backlog_inc(sch, skb); return NET_XMIT_SUCCESS; } static inline void __qdisc_enqueue_tail(struct sk_buff *skb, struct qdisc_skb_head *qh) { struct sk_buff *last = qh->tail; if (last) { skb->next = NULL; last->next = skb; // [6] qh->tail = skb; } else { qh->tail = skb; qh->head = skb; } qh->qlen++; } The skb pointer here is still not freed, but it will be just when the second fragmented packet arrives, leaving the dangling pointer as the last element of the queue. To be more precise, it will be freed in inet_frag_reasm_prepare with a consume_skb call (always starting from the tcf_classify call). # The primitives A first primitive might be already spotted from the shown code: static inline void __qdisc_enqueue_tail(struct sk_buff *skb, struct qdisc_skb_head *qh) { struct sk_buff *last = qh->tail; // [7] if (last) { skb->next = NULL; last->next = skb; // [6] qh->tail = skb; // [8] } else { qh->tail = skb; qh->head = skb; } qh->qlen++; } As we said, when the skb is erroneously inserted in the queue, it is still allocated but the ownership has been transferred. When the second fragment arrives, the skb is freed leaving a dangling pointer in the tail of the queue (qh->tail [7]), that is stored in the last variable and will lead to the primitive at [8] : a pointer write. In the qh->tail = skb line [8], qh->tail is a dangling pointer and, most importantly, we can make skb a dangling pointer too by using the same technique as before. And here there is an interesting detail from an exploitation point of view. When sending two fragmented packets, the first one's length is set to 0, while the second one carries the summed size of both packets. Why does this matter? Because, if you closely look at the if condition in bfifo_enqueue, packets are only enqueued if they do not exceed the scheduler limit (adjustable from user-space), meaning that we can deterministically place a pointer that we can then free with same bug primitive (e.g. the first fragments of the two), leading to a more interesting freed pointer write at offset 0 (next offset from the sk_buff struct). This is not the only primitive this bug offers. Another pretty powerful primitive come from the red_dequeue function (responsible to dequeue enqueued packets) and will be mentioned in the dedicated "Some notes on AI exploit development and the other primitive" chapter, with the reasons that led me choose this one instead. # The exploit # First assessment At this point I had a pretty limited UAF primitive and one and an half week until the pwn2own registration deadline, I had to make it quick. I spent some time in finding the most interesting object I could reallocate with a Cross Cache, overwrite the first 8 bytes, and exploit its side effects, wether another pointer, a size value (considering the big 0xffff... semi controlled value), a refcount or something similar. The only Cross Cache constraint is that the dangling pointer starts from an order 1 (2 pages) cache skbuff_head_cache (in systems with 4 cores or more). # First stage: from slab UAF to page UAF At some point I remembered about a public exploit that was targeting exactly sk_buff, and this is where I stumbled into the pgv struct, even if it was not using it like I would. static int packet_setsockopt(struct socket *sock, int level, int optname, sockptr_t optval, unsigned int optlen) { // .. switch (optname) { case PACKET_RX_RING: case PACKET_TX_RING: // [9] { // .. case TPACKET_V3: // .. if (!ret) ret = packet_set_ring(sk, &req_u, 0, optname == PACKET_TX_RING); release_sock(sk); return ret; } // .. } static int packet_set_ring(struct sock *sk, union tpacket_req_u *req_u, int closing, int tx_ring) { // .. if (req->tp_block_nr) { // .. order = get_order(req->tp_block_size); pg_vec = alloc_pg_vec(req, order); // .. } // .. } tatic struct pgv *alloc_pg_vec(struct tpacket_req *req, int order) { unsigned int block_nr = req->tp_block_nr; struct pgv *pg_vec; int i; pg_vec = kcalloc(block_nr, sizeof(struct pgv), GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_NOWARN); // [10] // .. for (i = 0; i < block_nr; i++) { pg_vec[i].buffer = alloc_one_pg_vec_page(order); // [11] if (unlikely(!pg_vec[i].buffer)) goto out_free_pgvec; } out: return pg_vec; // .. } static char *alloc_one_pg_vec_page(unsigned long order) { // .. buffer = (char *) __get_free_pages(gfp_flags, order); // [12] // .. } The pgv struct is incredibly interesting for its flexibility, but most importantly for its content. It can be allocated with a setsockopt syscall with PACKET_TX_RING [9] parameter, and it will allocate a user-controllable size object into an arbitrary generic cache (e.g. kmalloc-XXXX) given the user-controllable size specified in block_nr [10]. This allocation will be filled with page pointers [11] (allocated from __get_free_pages [12]) with the order value controllable from the user too. Since we can overwrite a pointer at offset 0, what's better than a list of page pointers? Before diving into the reallocation of the second object, let's see what primitives gives us the corruption of one of the pgv pointers with a dangling pointer. At first, I went down a rabbit hole that costed me an entire day and a bit more. It is possible, given an allocated pgv, to map it through the packet_mmap file operation. static int packet_mmap(struct file *file, struct socket *sock, struct vm_area_struct *vma) { // .. for (rb = &po->rx_ring; rb <= &po->tx_ring; rb++) { // .. for (i = 0; i < rb->pg_vec_len; i++) { void *kaddr = rb->pg_vec[i].buffer; // .. for (pg_num = 0; pg_num < rb->pg_vec_pages; pg_num++) { page = pgv_to_page(kaddr); err = vm_insert_page(vma, start, page); // [13] // .. } } } // .. } static int insert_page(struct vm_area_struct *vma, unsigned long addr, struct page *page, pgprot_t prot) { // .. retval = validate_page_before_insert(vma, page); if (retval) goto out; retval = -ENOMEM; pte = get_locked_pte(vma->vm_mm, addr, &ptl); if (!pte) goto out; retval = insert_page_into_pte_locked(vma, pte, addr, page, prot); pte_unmap_unlock(pte, ptl); out: return retval; } static int validate_page_before_insert(struct vm_area_struct *vma, struct page *page) { struct folio *folio = page_folio(page); // .. if (folio_test_anon(folio) || folio_test_slab(folio) || page_has_type(page)) return -EINVAL; flush_dcache_folio(folio); return 0; } As can be seen from the code, by calling mmap on that file descriptor, the mmap handler iterates over the just allocated pgv pointers and calls vm_insert_page [13] on each of them. Considering that we "control" one pointer, it might means that we can map to user-space an arbitrary pointer. And that's true, but not the entire truth. First, pgv_to_page calls virt_to_page and does a big favor to us by page aligning the pointer (considering that we have replaced a page aligned pointer with a slab pointer that might not be page aligned) and then calls vm_insert_page on it. At first, the idea was trying to map some arbitrary memory to user-space (e.g. an entire slab page, a read-only memory or file, ..) but this is not permitted due to sanity checks in validate_page_before_insert that validates that the pointer is not part of a slab and is not anonymous (e.g. is not file backed). I still think that something interesting can be done with this primitive, but it was taking too much time, the deadline was approaching and I had to make it somehow. Looking at other parts of the pgv code, I found another interesting primitive in the freeing process of the pgv pointers. While closing the socket file descriptor associated with the allocated pgv object, free_pg_vec iterates over each pointer and calls free_pages on each of them, permitting us to free our second dangling pointer again, but now with free_pages. This upgrades our primitive to a more powerful page-level UAF. And, similarly to the previous case, the pointer is page aligned directly inside the called free function. However, the time spent on the mmap path did not ended up as an entire waste of time, even if it was not chosen as the final primitive. It has been used in the exploit to verify which pgv allocation was containing the second dangling pointer instead of a legitimate pointer by mapping it with mmap and verifying if it succeed or not. If the mmap call fails, it means that vm_insert_page has failed and we obtain two useful information: the first stage has been successful and we know precisely which allocation has been corrupted! # Second stage: from page UAF to physical memory r/w Once the first dangling pointer is cross cached into a pgv object (I deliberately avoid details about cross cache, spraying and similar since these are well known techniques), we trigger the bug a second time to store the second dangling pointer at offset zero of the first cross cached dangling pointer (the pgv object). We then proceed to drain the skbuff_head_cache in order to move the second dangling pointer to the order 1 page per-cpu freelist, with the objective to exploit the just created page-level UAF. At this point I was trying multiple sprays to reclaim the page pointer with something interesting, and while I was spraying some anonymous regions, I found the holy grail from the gdb dump: 0x8000000114d1c067 0x8000000114d1c077 0x8000000114d1c087 0x8000000114d1c097 Look familiars? these are PTEs! Side note: you might be wondering how I was able to reclaim an order 1 allocation with an order-0 PTE allocation with just some "random" spray. Well, that's because at first I was writing the exploit in a 2 core qemu environment where 256-bytes slabs are order-0, the case of skbuff_head_cache. However, on systems with 4 or more cores, these slabs are order-1 and the exploit was targeting that configuration. I was immediately caught from that memory content, corrupting PTEs is an amazing primitive to ultimately achieve physical memory read and write. However, as mentioned, I was first writing the exploit against a qemu environment with 2 cores, making the skbuff_head_cache an order-0 cache instead of an order-1 (the pwn2own setup could be likely with more than 4 cores). This made me restructure the page reclamation process with some more shaping. At first, the second dangling pointer is drained from the skbuff_head_cache to the order-1 page per-cpu freelist. Then, in order to reallocate it as an order-0 allocation, the pointer must leave the page per-cpu freelist and be inserted into the order-1 buddy freelist, permitting it to be splitted into two order 0 allocations (hence reclaimed as a PTE). I have accomplished that by using multiple pgv allocations to spray and shape the page freelists. Then, PTEs have been sprayed through anonymous memory using mmap, and the order-1 allocation has been successfully reclaimed from two order-0 allocations. # Third stage: arbitrary physical memory read and write Once the second dangling pointer is reclaimed as a PTE, we can trigger the free_page on it and reallocate with something more controllable from user-space. The msg_msgseg object seemed a reasonable choice due to its high-level of control on content and lifetime from user-space (except for the first next pointer that will be NULL for order-0 allocations but that will not be a problem), even better than msg_msg. Another key aspect of this target object is that it will be allocated into a kmalloc- cache, making it easily readable and writable from user-space (considering the usercopy mitigation). At first, messages are sprayed with a leaked physical address (converted as PTE) from /sys/kernel/vmcoreinfo (readable from unprivileged users on all major distributions), to start with a valid physical address. To find the sprayed map that corresponds to the ovewritten PTEs, it is just necessary to verify the content of each mapped memory. Once the userland mapping has been found, in order to write new PTEs for the arbitrary physical read and write, we can receive and send all sprayed messages (msg_msgseg objects) one by one, to avoid the slab reallocation of different objects or even cache. During this process, it is also possible to find the correctly reallocated messages to optimize further PTEs modifications and have more stability. # Fourth stage: uid=0 At this point, it was two days before the registration deadline, I was really excited to have made it and I just needed the lasts, easier steps. I sent the registration e-mail and took a day off, with the idea of coming back once I got the registration and enjoy the engineering process of transforming the physical memory read and write to root. However, I didn't received any response the next day, neither in the deadline day, and neither later. Since I had a lot of other work to do, I just finished with this primitive. However, going from that really powerful primitive to root is just a matter of playing with it a bit. My first ideas were to scan memory starting from the vmcoreinfo leak (a dynamically allocated object, not part of any static kernel address), find a valid kernel address to break physical address randomization (enabled in Red Hat), and then overwrite the task_struct of the current process starting from the init_struct, or something similar. Unfortunately, I did not have the pleasure of doing that due to other pending projects with higher priority. # Some notes on AI exploit development and the other primitive During the exploitation process of this bug, since it could be considered a short-living bug given the pwn2own participation, I gave a try on using a public frontier model to write a full exploit: Opus 4.8 with Max Pro plan using Claude Code. I was surprised in both good and bad senses. The good part is that it effectively wrote a working exploit, using an alternative primitive from the red_dequeue code that permits to redirect execution and achieve a ROP chain from the sk_buff destructor. It was using a side channel for KASLR leak and pwn2own rules consider using it as a partial win, so I wanted something more self-contained with the same bug. I run multiple sessions with qemu and debugging capabilities given to the agent, but it was not able to find a valid KASLR leak from that primitive. The bad part instead is that it was not able to assist me much during the exploitation that I have explained in this article. I tried to redirect its thoughts with my ideas, giving some hints and some new paths, but after hours of background working, it was continuously falling back to the side-channel leak and ROP. At some point I was also doubting that my ideas would even work considering the continuous gaslighting from the model, but I had a sense that this bug could be more powerful and self-contained than what the model was saying. And, I was right. That said, this is just my experience, these model are extremely powerful and are continuously improving, as public articles show in the exploit development field, and a lot depends on how you use them, how many tokens and so many other factors. Also, no AI has been involved in writing this article. I found it pretty boring to find AI patterns in content mainly written for humans, that I prefer to see an imperfect, but personal and human recognizable, style of writing. If you see some errors, just ping me on X like it used to. # Conclusions I have not talked too much about spraying details, stabilization and reliability improvements, even if they were roughly 60-70% of the work. I wanted to focus more on general ideas of the exploitation strategy and bug root cause. I don't have plans to publish the exploit soon since the vulnerability is still pretty fresh, and there is also no CVE assigned at the moment of writing. About the pwn2own failed registration, I was not the only one with that experience, and I don't blame the organization. It was unfortunate and I was really excited to participate on it, considering that I have also posted about a failed attempt few years ago with Hacktive Security. However, AI has changed a lot the cat and mouse game of defensive/offensive, and it will take some time (maybe few years) to find a new balance and more calm waters. Maybe, or maybe not. # References https://ssd-disclosure.com/lpe-via-refcount-imbalance-in-the-af_unix-of-ubuntus-kernel/ https://www.hacktron.ai/blog/watching-gpt-55-sol-ultra-write-a-chrome-exploit-exploit-development-as-we-know-it-is-over

blocks.html_en: This article is related to a Linux Kernel 0-day I have recently found and exploited initially for Pwn2own 2026 (Red Hat category). Unfortunately, I waited too long to register (even if it was before the deadline) and ended up being ignored (legitimately due to the unexpected number of submissions) and so, I was not able to participate. The bug ended up being reported from another researcher, but no public details (and no CVE) have been still published about it and on its exploitability. The bug is just another bug in the network scheduler subsystem, the red scheduler. I have picked this subsystem as a target for three main reasons: unprivileged user namespaces are enabled in Red Hat, schedulers are an historically interesting subsystem (due to the high complexity and interactions with multiple network parts) and I had a fuzzer suitable for that kind of targets (syzkaller has its own limitations with this type of subsystems). Besides of the bug, the most interesting part is the exploitation strategy, turning an initially limited slab UAF into a page UAF and ultimately into a physical memory read and write.

sentences.en: This is where the bug lives, and is simply a missing case for the TC_ACT_CONSUMED value that can be returned from tcf_classify [3].
sentences.zh: 这就是 bug 存在的地方，并且只是可从 tcf_classify [3] 返回的 TC_ACT_CONSUMED 值缺失的情况。

sentences.en: This value can be returned in multiple user controllable scenarios (e.g. when a fragmented packet is sent) and basically means that the ownership is transferred to the skb defragmentation engine, and the caller has to drop it.
sentences.zh: 该值可以在多个用户可控场景中返回（例如，当发送分片数据包时），基本上意味着所有权已转移到 skb 碎片整理引擎，调用者必须丢弃它。

sentences.en: tcf_ct_act is called (after few dereferences) from tcf_classify and returns TC_ACT_CONSUMED [4] when tcf_ct_handle_fragments returns something, that is the case for a first fragmented packet sent.
sentences.zh: tcf_ct_act 从 tcf_classify 调用（经过几次解引用后），并在 tcf_ct_handle_fragments 返回某些内容时返回 TC_ACT_CONSUMED [4]，这是发送第一个分段数据包的情况。

sentences.en: However, the switch condition in tcf_qevent_handle ignores the TC_ACT_CONSUMED case and fallthrough into returning the skb instead of NULL , ultimately ending up into enqueueing the packet in the scheduler queue [5] with qdisc_enqueue, indirectly calling bfifo_enqueue that will store the skb pointer (still not freed) as the last element of its queue [6] using the skb->next list.
sentences.zh: 然而， tcf_qevent_handle 中的切换条件忽略 TC_ACT_CONSUMED 情况，并返回 skb 而不是 NULL ，最终导致使用 qdisc_enqueue 将数据包排入调度程序队列 [5] 中，间接调用 bfifo_enqueue ，该 bfifo_enqueue 将使用 skb->next 列表将 skb 指针（仍未释放）存储为其队列 [6] 的最后一个元素。

blocks.html_en: The skb pointer here is still not freed, but it will be just when the second fragmented packet arrives, leaving the dangling pointer as the last element of the queue. To be more precise, it will be freed in inet_frag_reasm_prepare with a consume_skb call (always starting from the tcf_classify call).

sentences.en: The skb pointer here is still not freed, but it will be just when the second fragmented packet arrives, leaving the dangling pointer as the last element of the queue.
sentences.zh: 这里的 skb 指针仍然没有被释放，但它会在第二个分片数据包到达时释放，从而使悬空指针成为队列的最后一个元素。

sentences.en: To be more precise, it will be freed in inet_frag_reasm_prepare with a consume_skb call (always starting from the tcf_classify call).
sentences.zh: 更准确地说，它将在 inet_frag_reasm_prepare 中通过 Consumer_skb 调用释放（始终从 tcf_classify 调用开始）。

blocks.html_en: As we said, when the skb is erroneously inserted in the queue, it is still allocated but the ownership has been transferred. When the second fragment arrives, the skb is freed leaving a dangling pointer in the tail of the queue (qh->tail [7]), that is stored in the last variable and will lead to the primitive at [8] : a pointer write.

sentences.en: As we said, when the skb is erroneously inserted in the queue, it is still allocated but the ownership has been transferred.
sentences.zh: 正如我们所说，当skb被错误地插入队列时，它仍然被分配，但所有权已经转移。

sentences.en: When the second fragment arrives, the skb is freed leaving a dangling pointer in the tail of the queue (qh->tail [7]), that is stored in the last variable and will lead to the primitive at [8] : a pointer write.
sentences.zh: 当第二个片段到达时，skb 被释放，在队列尾部留下一个悬空指针 (qh->tail [7])，该指针存储在最后一个变量中，并将导致 [8] 处的原语：指针写入。

blocks.html_en: In the qh->tail = skb line [8], qh->tail is a dangling pointer and, most importantly, we can make skb a dangling pointer too by using the same technique as before. And here there is an interesting detail from an exploitation point of view. When sending two fragmented packets, the first one's length is set to 0, while the second one carries the summed size of both packets. Why does this matter? Because, if you closely look at the if condition in bfifo_enqueue, packets are only enqueued if they do not exceed the scheduler limit (adjustable from user-space), meaning that we can deterministically place a pointer that we can then free with same bug primitive (e.g. the first fragments of the two), leading to a more interesting freed pointer write at offset 0 (next offset from the sk_buff struct).

sentences.en: In the qh->tail = skb line [8], qh->tail is a dangling pointer and, most importantly, we can make skb a dangling pointer too by using the same technique as before.
sentences.zh: 在 qh->tail = skb 行 [8] 中，qh->tail 是一个悬空指针，最重要的是，我们可以使用与之前相同的技术使 skb 也成为悬空指针。

sentences.en: And here there is an interesting detail from an exploitation point of view.
sentences.zh: 从利用的角度来看，这里有一个有趣的细节。

sentences.en: When sending two fragmented packets, the first one's length is set to 0, while the second one carries the summed size of both packets.
sentences.zh: 当发送两个分片数据包时，第一个数据包的长度设置为 0，而第二个数据包携带两个数据包的总大小。

sentences.en: Why does this matter?
sentences.zh: 为什么这很重要？

sentences.en: Because, if you closely look at the if condition in bfifo_enqueue, packets are only enqueued if they do not exceed the scheduler limit (adjustable from user-space), meaning that we can deterministically place a pointer that we can then free with same bug primitive (e.g. the first fragments of the two), leading to a more interesting freed pointer write at offset 0 (next offset from the sk_buff struct).
sentences.zh: 因为，如果仔细观察 bfifo_enqueue 中的 if 条件，数据包只有在不超过调度程序限制（可从用户空间调整）时才会入队，这意味着我们可以确定性地放置一个指针，然后可以使用相同的 bug 原语（例如两者的第一个片段）释放该指针，从而导致在偏移量 0（从 sk_buff 结构体开始的下一个偏移量）处写入更有趣的释放指针。

blocks.html_en: This is not the only primitive this bug offers. Another pretty powerful primitive come from the red_dequeue function (responsible to dequeue enqueued packets) and will be mentioned in the dedicated "Some notes on AI exploit development and the other primitive" chapter, with the reasons that led me choose this one instead.

The only Cross Cache constraint is that the dangling pointer starts from an order 1 (2 pages) cache skbuff_head_cache (in systems with 4 cores or more).

blocks.html_en: At some point I remembered about a public exploit that was targeting exactly sk_buff, and this is where I stumbled into the pgv struct, even if it was not using it like I would.

sentences.en: At some point I remembered about a public exploit that was targeting exactly sk_buff, and this is where I stumbled into the pgv struct, even if it was not using it like I would.
sentences.zh: 在某个时候，我记得有一个针对 sk_buff 的公开漏洞，这就是我偶然发现 pgv 结构的地方，即使它没有像我那样使用它。

blocks.html_en: The pgv struct is incredibly interesting for its flexibility, but most importantly for its content. It can be allocated with a setsockopt syscall with PACKET_TX_RING [9] parameter, and it will allocate a user-controllable size object into an arbitrary generic cache (e.g. kmalloc-XXXX) given the user-controllable size specified in block_nr [10]. This allocation will be filled with page pointers [11] (allocated from __get_free_pages [12]) with the order value controllable from the user too. Since we can overwrite a pointer at offset 0, what's better than a list of page pointers?

sentences.en: The pgv struct is incredibly interesting for its flexibility, but most importantly for its content.
sentences.zh: pgv 结构因其灵活性而非常有趣，但最重要的是其内容。

sentences.en: It can be allocated with a setsockopt syscall with PACKET_TX_RING [9] parameter, and it will allocate a user-controllable size object into an arbitrary generic cache (e.g. kmalloc-XXXX) given the user-controllable size specified in block_nr [10].
sentences.zh: 它可以通过带有 PACKET_TX_RING [9] 参数的 setsockopt 系统调用进行分配，并且根据 block_nr [10] 中指定的用户可控大小，它将将用户可控大小的对象分配到任意通用缓存（例如 kmalloc-XXXX）中。

sentences.en: This allocation will be filled with page pointers [11] (allocated from __get_free_pages [12]) with the order value controllable from the user too.
sentences.zh: 该分配将填充页面指针 [11]（从 __get_free_pages [12] 分配），其顺序值也可由用户控制。

sentences.en: Since we can overwrite a pointer at offset 0, what's better than a list of page pointers?
sentences.zh: 既然我们可以覆盖偏移量 0 处的指针，那么还有什么比页指针列表更好呢？

blocks.html_en: Before diving into the reallocation of the second object, let's see what primitives gives us the corruption of one of the pgv pointers with a dangling pointer.

sentences.en: Before diving into the reallocation of the second object, let's see what primitives gives us the corruption of one of the pgv pointers with a dangling pointer.
sentences.zh: 在深入研究第二个对象的重新分配之前，让我们看看哪些原语导致 pgv 指针之一带有悬空指针的损坏。

blocks.html_en: At first, I went down a rabbit hole that costed me an entire day and a bit more. It is possible, given an allocated pgv, to map it through the packet_mmap file operation.

sentences.en: At first, I went down a rabbit hole that costed me an entire day and a bit more.
sentences.zh: 起初，我掉进了一个兔子洞，花了我一整天甚至更多的时间。

sentences.en: It is possible, given an allocated pgv, to map it through the packet_mmap file operation.
sentences.zh: 给定一个已分配的 pgv，可以通过 packet_mmap 文件操作来映射它。

blocks.html_en: As can be seen from the code, by calling mmap on that file descriptor, the mmap handler iterates over the just allocated pgv pointers and calls vm_insert_page [13] on each of them. Considering that we "control" one pointer, it might means that we can map to user-space an arbitrary pointer. And that's true, but not the entire truth.

sentences.en: As can be seen from the code, by calling mmap on that file descriptor, the mmap handler iterates over the just allocated pgv pointers and calls vm_insert_page [13] on each of them.
sentences.zh: 从代码中可以看出，通过在该文件描述符上调用 mmap，mmap 处理程序会迭代刚刚分配的 pgv 指针，并对每个指针调用 vm_insert_page [13]。

sentences.en: Considering that we "control" one pointer, it might means that we can map to user-space an arbitrary pointer.
sentences.zh: 考虑到我们“控制”一个指针，这可能意味着我们可以将任意指针映射到用户空间。

sentences.en: And that's true, but not the entire truth.
sentences.zh: 这是事实，但不是全部事实。

blocks.html_en: First, pgv_to_page calls virt_to_page and does a big favor to us by page aligning the pointer (considering that we have replaced a page aligned pointer with a slab pointer that might not be page aligned) and then calls vm_insert_page on it. At first, the idea was trying to map some arbitrary memory to user-space (e.g. an entire slab page, a read-only memory or file, ..) but this is not permitted due to sanity checks in validate_page_before_insert that validates that the pointer is not part of a slab and is not anonymous (e.g. is not file backed). I still think that something interesting can be done with this primitive, but it was taking too much time, the deadline was approaching and I had to make it somehow.

sentences.en: First, pgv_to_page calls virt_to_page and does a big favor to us by page aligning the pointer (considering that we have replaced a page aligned pointer with a slab pointer that might not be page aligned) and then calls vm_insert_page on it.
sentences.zh: 首先，pgv_to_page 调用 virt_to_page 并通过页对齐指针（考虑到我们已经用可能不是页对齐的平板指针替换了页对齐指针）为我们带来了很大帮助，然后在其上调用 vm_insert_page 。

sentences.en: At first, the idea was trying to map some arbitrary memory to user-space (e.g. an entire slab page, a read-only memory or file, ..)
sentences.zh: 起初，这个想法是尝试将一些任意内存映射到用户空间（例如整个平板页、只读内存或文件，..）

sentences.en: but this is not permitted due to sanity checks in validate_page_before_insert that validates that the pointer is not part of a slab and is not anonymous (e.g. is not file backed).
sentences.zh: 但这是不允许的，因为 validate_page_before_insert 中的健全性检查验证指针不是板的一部分并且不是匿名的（例如不是文件支持的）。

sentences.en: I still think that something interesting can be done with this primitive, but it was taking too much time, the deadline was approaching and I had to make it somehow.
sentences.zh: 我仍然认为可以用这个原语做一些有趣的事情，但是它花费了太多时间，截止日期即将到来，我必须以某种方式完成它。

blocks.html_en: Looking at other parts of the pgv code, I found another interesting primitive in the freeing process of the pgv pointers. While closing the socket file descriptor associated with the allocated pgv object, free_pg_vec iterates over each pointer and calls free_pages on each of them, permitting us to free our second dangling pointer again, but now with free_pages. This upgrades our primitive to a more powerful page-level UAF. And, similarly to the previous case, the pointer is page aligned directly inside the called free function.

sentences.en: Looking at other parts of the pgv code, I found another interesting primitive in the freeing process of the pgv pointers.
sentences.zh: 查看 pgv 代码的其他部分，我在 pgv 指针的释放过程中发现了另一个有趣的原语。

sentences.en: While closing the socket file descriptor associated with the allocated pgv object, free_pg_vec iterates over each pointer and calls free_pages on each of them, permitting us to free our second dangling pointer again, but now with free_pages.
sentences.zh: 在关闭与分配的 pgv 对象关联的套接字文件描述符时， free_pg_vec 迭代每个指针并在每个指针上调用 free_pages ，允许我们再次释放第二个悬空指针，但现在使用 free_pages 。

sentences.en: This upgrades our primitive to a more powerful page-level UAF.
sentences.zh: 这将我们的原语升级为更强大的页面级 UAF。

sentences.en: And, similarly to the previous case, the pointer is page aligned directly inside the called free function.
sentences.zh: 并且，与前面的情况类似，指针直接在被调用的自由函数内部进行页面对齐。

blocks.html_en: However, the time spent on the mmap path did not ended up as an entire waste of time, even if it was not chosen as the final primitive. It has been used in the exploit to verify which pgv allocation was containing the second dangling pointer instead of a legitimate pointer by mapping it with mmap and verifying if it succeed or not. If the mmap call fails, it means that vm_insert_page has failed and we obtain two useful information: the first stage has been successful and we know precisely which allocation has been corrupted!

We then proceed to drain the skbuff_head_cache in order to move the second dangling pointer to the order 1 page per-cpu freelist, with the objective to exploit the just created page-level UAF.

blocks.html_en: Side note: you might be wondering how I was able to reclaim an order 1 allocation with an order-0 PTE allocation with just some "random" spray. Well, that's because at first I was writing the exploit in a 2 core qemu environment where 256-bytes slabs are order-0, the case of skbuff_head_cache. However, on systems with 4 or more cores, these slabs are order-1 and the exploit was targeting that configuration.

blocks.html_en: I was immediately caught from that memory content, corrupting PTEs is an amazing primitive to ultimately achieve physical memory read and write.

sentences.en: I was immediately caught from that memory content, corrupting PTEs is an amazing primitive to ultimately achieve physical memory read and write.

blocks.html_en: However, as mentioned, I was first writing the exploit against a qemu environment with 2 cores, making the skbuff_head_cache an order-0 cache instead of an order-1 (the pwn2own setup could be likely with more than 4 cores). This made me restructure the page reclamation process with some more shaping. At first, the second dangling pointer is drained from the skbuff_head_cache to the order-1 page per-cpu freelist. Then, in order to reallocate it as an order-0 allocation, the pointer must leave the page per-cpu freelist and be inserted into the order-1 buddy freelist, permitting it to be splitted into two order 0 allocations (hence reclaimed as a PTE). I have accomplished that by using multiple pgv allocations to spray and shape the page freelists. Then, PTEs have been sprayed through anonymous memory using mmap, and the order-1 allocation has been successfully reclaimed from two order-0 allocations.

sentences.en: However, as mentioned, I was first writing the exploit against a qemu environment with 2 cores, making the skbuff_head_cache an order-0 cache instead of an order-1 (the pwn2own setup could be likely with more than 4 cores).

sentences.zh: 这让我通过更多的调整来重新构建页面回收过程。

sentences.en: At first, the second dangling pointer is drained from the skbuff_head_cache to the order-1 page per-cpu freelist.
sentences.zh: 首先，第二个悬空指针从 skbuff_head_cache 排出到每 cpu 的 order-1 页空闲列表。

sentences.en: Then, in order to reallocate it as an order-0 allocation, the pointer must leave the page per-cpu freelist and be inserted into the order-1 buddy freelist, permitting it to be splitted into two order 0 allocations (hence reclaimed as a PTE).
sentences.zh: 然后，为了将其重新分配为 0 阶分配，指针必须离开每 cpu 空闲列表页面并插入到 1 阶好友空闲列表中，从而允许将其拆分为两个 0 阶分配（因此作为 PTE 回收）。

sentences.en: I have accomplished that by using multiple pgv allocations to spray and shape the page freelists.
sentences.zh: 我通过使用多个 pgv 分配来喷射和调整页面空闲列表来实现这一点。

sentences.en: Then, PTEs have been sprayed through anonymous memory using mmap, and the order-1 allocation has been successfully reclaimed from two order-0 allocations.
sentences.zh: 然后，使用 mmap 通过匿名内存喷射 PTE，并且已成功从两个 0 阶分配中回收了 1 阶分配。

blocks.html_en: # Third stage: arbitrary physical memory read and write

sentences.en: # Third stage: arbitrary physical memory read and write
sentences.zh: # 第三阶段：任意物理内存读写

blocks.html_en: Once the second dangling pointer is reclaimed as a PTE, we can trigger the free_page on it and reallocate with something more controllable from user-space. The msg_msgseg object seemed a reasonable choice due to its high-level of control on content and lifetime from user-space (except for the first next pointer that will be NULL for order-0 allocations but that will not be a problem), even better than msg_msg. Another key aspect of this target object is that it will be allocated into a kmalloc- cache, making it easily readable and writable from user-space (considering the usercopy mitigation).

sentences.en: Once the second dangling pointer is reclaimed as a PTE, we can trigger the free_page on it and reallocate with something more controllable from user-space.
sentences.zh: 一旦第二个悬空指针被回收为 PTE，我们就可以触发其上的 free_page 并用用户空间更可控的东西重新分配。

sentences.en: The msg_msgseg object seemed a reasonable choice due to its high-level of control on content and lifetime from user-space (except for the first next pointer that will be NULL for order-0 allocations but that will not be a problem), even better than msg_msg.
sentences.zh: msg_msgseg 对象似乎是一个合理的选择，因为它对用户空间的内容和生命周期进行了高级别的控制（除了第一个下一个指针对于 order-0 分配将为 NULL，但这不会成为问题），甚至比 msg_msg 更好。

sentences.en: Another key aspect of this target object is that it will be allocated into a kmalloc- cache, making it easily readable and writable from user-space (considering the usercopy mitigation).
sentences.zh: 该目标对象的另一个关键方面是它将被分配到 kmalloc-cache 中，从而使其可以轻松地从用户空间读取和写入（考虑用户复制缓解）。

blocks.html_en: At first, messages are sprayed with a leaked physical address (converted as PTE) from /sys/kernel/vmcoreinfo (readable from unprivileged users on all major distributions), to start with a valid physical address. To find the sprayed map that corresponds to the ovewritten PTEs, it is just necessary to verify the content of each mapped memory. Once the userland mapping has been found, in order to write new PTEs for the arbitrary physical read and write, we can receive and send all sprayed messages (msg_msgseg objects) one by one, to avoid the slab reallocation of different objects or even cache. During this process, it is also possible to find the correctly reallocated messages to optimize further PTEs modifications and have more stability.

sentences.en: At first, messages are sprayed with a leaked physical address (converted as PTE) from /sys/kernel/vmcoreinfo (readable from unprivileged users on all major distributions), to start with a valid physical address.
sentences.zh: 首先，消息会使用 /sys/kernel/vmcoreinfo（所有主要发行版上的非特权用户均可读取）中泄露的物理地址（转换为 PTE）进行喷射，以有效的物理地址开始。

sentences.en: To find the sprayed map that corresponds to the ovewritten PTEs, it is just necessary to verify the content of each mapped memory.
sentences.zh: 为了找到与被覆盖的PTE对应的喷射映射，只需验证每个映射内存的内容即可。

sentences.en: Once the userland mapping has been found, in order to write new PTEs for the arbitrary physical read and write, we can receive and send all sprayed messages (msg_msgseg objects) one by one, to avoid the slab reallocation of different objects or even cache.
sentences.zh: 一旦找到用户态映射，为了为任意物理读写写入新的PTE，我们可以一一接收和发送所有喷射消息（msg_msgseg对象），以避免不同对象甚至缓存的slab重新分配。

sentences.en: During this process, it is also possible to find the correctly reallocated messages to optimize further PTEs modifications and have more stability.
sentences.zh: 在此过程中，还可以找到正确重新分配的消息，以优化进一步的 PTE 修改并具有更高的稳定性。

I sent the registration e-mail and took a day off, with the idea of coming back once I got the registration and enjoy the engineering process of transforming the physical memory read and write to root. However, I didn't received any response the next day, neither in the deadline day, and neither later. Since I had a lot of other work to do, I just finished with this primitive.

blocks.html_en: However, going from that really powerful primitive to root is just a matter of playing with it a bit. My first ideas were to scan memory starting from the vmcoreinfo leak (a dynamically allocated object, not part of any static kernel address), find a valid kernel address to break physical address randomization (enabled in Red Hat), and then overwrite the task_struct of the current process starting from the init_struct, or something similar. Unfortunately, I did not have the pleasure of doing that due to other pending projects with higher priority.

blocks.html_en: During the exploitation process of this bug, since it could be considered a short-living bug given the pwn2own participation, I gave a try on using a public frontier model to write a full exploit: Opus 4.8 with Max Pro plan using Claude Code. I was surprised in both good and bad senses.

sentences.zh: 在这个漏洞的利用过程中，由于考虑到pwn2own的参与，它可以被认为是一个短暂的漏洞，因此我尝试使用公共前沿模型来编写完整的漏洞利用：Opus 4.8 with Max Pro plan using Claude Code。

sentences.en: I was surprised in both good and bad senses.
sentences.zh: 我无论从好的方面还是坏的方面都感到惊讶。

blocks.html_en: The good part is that it effectively wrote a working exploit, using an alternative primitive from the red_dequeue code that permits to redirect execution and achieve a ROP chain from the sk_buff destructor. It was using a side channel for KASLR leak and pwn2own rules consider using it as a partial win, so I wanted something more self-contained with the same bug. I run multiple sessions with qemu and debugging capabilities given to the agent, but it was not able to find a valid KASLR leak from that primitive.

blocks.html_en: The bad part instead is that it was not able to assist me much during the exploitation that I have explained in this article. I tried to redirect its thoughts with my ideas, giving some hints and some new paths, but after hours of background working, it was continuously falling back to the side-channel leak and ROP. At some point I was also doubting that my ideas would even work considering the continuous gaslighting from the model, but I had a sense that this bug could be more powerful and self-contained than what the model was saying. And, I was right.

blocks.html_en: That said, this is just my experience, these model are extremely powerful and are continuously improving, as public articles show in the exploit development field, and a lot depends on how you use them, how many tokens and so many other factors.

content_html_zh:
2026 - @kiks

  1. 01简介
  2. 02错误
  3. 03原语
  4. 04利用
  5. 第一次评估
  6. 第一阶段：从slab UAF到page UAF
  7. 第二阶段：从页UAF到物理内存r/w
  8. 第三阶段：任意物理内存读写
  9. 第四阶段：uid=0
  10. 05关于人工智能漏洞利用开发和其他原语的一些注释
  11. 结论
  12. 06参考文献

＃ 介绍

本文与我最近发现并最初用于 Pwn2own 2026（红帽类别）的 Linux 内核 0day 相关。 不幸的是，我报名的时间太长了（即使是在截止日期之前），最终被忽略了（由于提交的数量超出预期），所以我无法参加。 该错误最终由另一位研究人员报告，但尚未发布有关该错误及其可利用性的公开详细信息（也没有 CVE）。 该错误只是网络调度程序子系统（红色调度程序）中的另一个错误。 我选择这个子系统作为目标有三个主要原因：红帽中启用了非特权用户命名空间，调度程序是一个历史上有趣的子系统（由于高度复杂性和与多个网络部分的交互），并且我有一个适合此类目标的模糊器（syzkaller 对于此类子系统有其自身的局限性）。 除了bug之外，最有趣的部分是利用策略，将最初有限的slab UAF转变为页面UAF，并最终转变为物理内存读写。

# 错误

该漏洞影响“红色网络调度程序”（这真是巧合，因为我的目标是红帽），并已在提交 3f14b37（2024 年 1 月）中引入，并在提交 a8a0289（2026 年 6 月）中修复，持续了 2.5 年。 修复方法非常简单且直接：

--- a/net/sched/cls_api.c
+++ b/net/sched/cls_api.c
@@ -4049,6 +4049,9 @@ struct sk_buff *tcf_qevent_handle(struct tcf_qevent *qe, struct Qdisc *sch, stru
        skb_do_redirect(skb);
        *ret = __NET_XMIT_STOLEN;
        return NULL;
+   case TC_ACT_CONSUMED:
+       *ret = __NET_XMIT_STOLEN;
+       return NULL;
    }

    return skb;

我无法比提交中已经完成的内容更好地解释，所以我只是从中提取关键部分并在顶部添加更多详细信息：

tcf_classify() can return TC_ACT_CONSUMED while the skb is held by the
defragmentation engine (e.g. act_ct on out-of-order fragments). When
that happens the skb is no longer owned by the caller and must not be
touched again.

tcf_qevent_handle() did not handle TC_ACT_CONSUMED: it fell through the
switch and returned the skb to the caller as if classification had
passed.

通过代码在实践中看到它，红色入队函数（red_enqueue）负责将数据包入队到它自己的调度程序上。

static int red_enqueue(struct sk_buff *skb, struct Qdisc *sch,
               struct sk_buff **to_free)
{
    switch (red_action(&q->parms, &q->vars, q->vars.qavg)) { // [2]
    case RED_DONT_MARK:
        break;

    case RED_PROB_MARK:
        // ..
        if (INET_ECN_set_ce(skb)) {
            q->stats.prob_mark++;
            skb = tcf_qevent_handle(&q->qe_mark, sch, skb, to_free, &ret); // [1]
            if (!skb)
                return NET_XMIT_CN | ret;
            // ..
        /* Non-ECT packet in ECN nodrop mode: queue it. */
        break;
    case RED_HARD_MARK:
        // ..
        if (INET_ECN_set_ce(skb)) {
            q->stats.forced_mark++;
            skb = tcf_qevent_handle(&q->qe_mark, sch, skb, to_free, &ret); // [1]
            if (!skb)
                return NET_XMIT_CN | ret;
        // ..
    }
    // ..
    ret = qdisc_enqueue(skb, child, to_free); // [5]
    // ..
}

当 red_action [2] 返回 RED_PROB_MARK 或 RED_HARD_MARK 时（基本上是在达到用户可控阈值时），tcf_qevent_handle [1] 函数会在多个位置被调用。 当返回两个值之一并设置 ECN（可从 tc 命令行配置）时，将调用 tcf_qevent_handle：

struct sk_buff *tcf_qevent_handle(struct tcf_qevent *qe, struct Qdisc *sch, struct sk_buff *skb,
                  struct sk_buff **to_free, int *ret)
{
    // ..
    switch (tcf_classify(skb, NULL, fl, &cl_res, false)) { // [3]
    case TC_ACT_SHOT:
        qdisc_qstats_drop(sch);
        __qdisc_drop(skb, to_free);
        *ret = __NET_XMIT_BYPASS;
        return NULL;
    case TC_ACT_STOLEN:
    case TC_ACT_QUEUED:
    case TC_ACT_TRAP:
        __qdisc_drop(skb, to_free);
        *ret = __NET_XMIT_STOLEN;
        return NULL;
    case TC_ACT_REDIRECT:
        skb_do_redirect(skb);
        *ret = __NET_XMIT_STOLEN;
        return NULL;
    }

    return skb;
}

// called from tcf_classify after few dereferences
TC_INDIRECT_SCOPE int tcf_ct_act(struct sk_buff *skb, const struct tc_action *a,
                 struct tcf_result *res)
{
    // ..
    err = tcf_ct_handle_fragments(net, skb, family, p->zone, &defrag);
    if (err)
        goto out_frag;

    // ..
out_frag:
    if (err != -EINPROGRESS)
        tcf_action_inc_drop_qstats(&c->common);
    return TC_ACT_CONSUMED; // [4]
}

这就是 bug 存在的地方，并且只是可从 tcf_classify [3] 返回的 TC_ACT_CONSUMED 值缺失的情况。 该值可以在多个用户可控场景中返回（例如，当发送分片数据包时），基本上意味着所有权已转移到 skb 碎片整理引擎，调用者必须丢弃它。 tcf_ct_act 从 tcf_classify 调用（经过几次解引用后），并在 tcf_ct_handle_fragments 返回某些内容时返回 TC_ACT_CONSUMED [4]，这是发送第一个分段数据包的情况。 然而， tcf_qevent_handle 中的切换条件忽略 TC_ACT_CONSUMED 情况，并返回 skb 而不是 NULL ，最终导致使用 qdisc_enqueue 将数据包排入调度程序队列 [5] 中，间接调用 bfifo_enqueue ，该 bfifo_enqueue 将使用 skb->next 列表将 skb 指针（仍未释放）存储为其队列 [6] 的最后一个元素。

static int bfifo_enqueue(struct sk_buff *skb, struct Qdisc *sch,
             struct sk_buff **to_free)
{
    if (likely(sch->qstats.backlog + qdisc_pkt_len(skb) <=
           READ_ONCE(sch->limit)))
        return qdisc_enqueue_tail(skb, sch);

    return qdisc_drop(skb, sch, to_free);
}

static inline int qdisc_enqueue_tail(struct sk_buff *skb, struct Qdisc *sch)
{
    __qdisc_enqueue_tail(skb, &sch->q);
    qdisc_qstats_backlog_inc(sch, skb);
    return NET_XMIT_SUCCESS;
}

static inline void __qdisc_enqueue_tail(struct sk_buff *skb,
                    struct qdisc_skb_head *qh)
{
    struct sk_buff *last = qh->tail;

    if (last) {
        skb->next = NULL;
        last->next = skb; // [6]
        qh->tail = skb;
    } else {
        qh->tail = skb;
        qh->head = skb;
    }
    qh->qlen++;
}

这里的 skb 指针仍然没有被释放，但它会在第二个分片数据包到达时释放，从而使悬空指针成为队列的最后一个元素。 更准确地说，它将在 inet_frag_reasm_prepare 中通过 Consumer_skb 调用释放（始终从 tcf_classify 调用开始）。

# 原语

第一个原语可能已经从显示的代码中发现：

static inline void __qdisc_enqueue_tail(struct sk_buff *skb,
                    struct qdisc_skb_head *qh)
{
    struct sk_buff *last = qh->tail; // [7]

    if (last) {
        skb->next = NULL;
        last->next = skb; // [6]
        qh->tail = skb;   // [8]
    } else {
        qh->tail = skb;
        qh->head = skb;
    }
    qh->qlen++;
}

正如我们所说，当skb被错误地插入队列时，它仍然被分配，但所有权已经转移。 当第二个片段到达时，skb 被释放，在队列尾部留下一个悬空指针 (qh->tail [7])，该指针存储在最后一个变量中，并将导致 [8] 处的原语：指针写入。

在 qh->tail = skb 行 [8] 中，qh->tail 是一个悬空指针，最重要的是，我们可以使用与之前相同的技术使 skb 也成为悬空指针。 从利用的角度来看，这里有一个有趣的细节。 当发送两个分片数据包时，第一个数据包的长度设置为 0，而第二个数据包携带两个数据包的总大小。 为什么这很重要？ 因为，如果仔细观察 bfifo_enqueue 中的 if 条件，数据包只有在不超过调度程序限制（可从用户空间调整）时才会入队，这意味着我们可以确定性地放置一个指针，然后可以使用相同的 bug 原语（例如两者的第一个片段）释放该指针，从而导致在偏移量 0（从 sk_buff 结构体开始的下一个偏移量）处写入更有趣的释放指针。

这并不是该错误提供的唯一原语。 另一个非常强大的原语来自 red_dequeue 函数（负责使已排队的数据包出队），并将在专门的“关于 AI 漏洞利用开发和其他原语的一些注释”章节中提到，以及导致我选择这个原语的原因。

# 漏洞利用

# 第一次评估

此时我的 UAF 原语非常有限，距离 pwn2own 注册截止日期还有一个半星期，我必须尽快完成。 我花了一些时间寻找最有趣的对象，我可以使用交叉缓存重新分配，覆盖前 8 个字节，并利用它的副作用，无论是另一个指针，大小值（考虑大 0xffff ... 半控制值）、重新计数或类似的东西。 唯一的跨缓存约束是悬空指针从 1 阶（2 页）缓存 skbuff_head_cache 开始（在具有 4 个或更多核心的系统中）。

# 第一阶段：从slab UAF到page UAF

在某个时候，我记得有一个针对 sk_buff 的公开漏洞，这就是我偶然发现 pgv 结构的地方，即使它没有像我那样使用它。

static int
packet_setsockopt(struct socket *sock, int level, int optname, sockptr_t optval,
          unsigned int optlen)
{
    // ..
    switch (optname) {
    case PACKET_RX_RING:
    case PACKET_TX_RING: // [9]
    {
        // ..
        case TPACKET_V3:
        // ..
        if (!ret)
            ret = packet_set_ring(sk, &req_u, 0,
                          optname == PACKET_TX_RING);
        release_sock(sk);
        return ret;
    }
    // ..
}

static int packet_set_ring(struct sock *sk, union tpacket_req_u *req_u,
        int closing, int tx_ring)
{
    // ..
    if (req->tp_block_nr) {
        // ..
        order = get_order(req->tp_block_size);
        pg_vec = alloc_pg_vec(req, order);
        // ..
    }
    // ..
}

tatic struct pgv *alloc_pg_vec(struct tpacket_req *req, int order)
{
    unsigned int block_nr = req->tp_block_nr;
    struct pgv *pg_vec;
    int i;

    pg_vec = kcalloc(block_nr, sizeof(struct pgv), GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_NOWARN); // [10]
    // ..

    for (i = 0; i < block_nr; i++) {
        pg_vec[i].buffer = alloc_one_pg_vec_page(order); // [11]
        if (unlikely(!pg_vec[i].buffer))
            goto out_free_pgvec;
    }
out:
    return pg_vec;
    // ..
}

static char *alloc_one_pg_vec_page(unsigned long order)
{
    // ..
    buffer = (char *) __get_free_pages(gfp_flags, order); // [12]
    // ..
}

pgv 结构因其灵活性而非常有趣，但最重要的是其内容。 它可以通过带有 PACKET_TX_RING [9] 参数的 setsockopt 系统调用进行分配，并且根据 block_nr [10] 中指定的用户可控大小，它将将用户可控大小的对象分配到任意通用缓存（例如 kmalloc-XXXX）中。 该分配将填充页面指针 [11]（从 __get_free_pages [12] 分配），其顺序值也可由用户控制。 既然我们可以覆盖偏移量 0 处的指针，那么还有什么比页指针列表更好呢？

在深入研究第二个对象的重新分配之前，让我们看看哪些原语导致 pgv 指针之一带有悬空指针的损坏。

起初，我掉进了一个兔子洞，花了我一整天甚至更多的时间。 给定一个已分配的 pgv，可以通过 packet_mmap 文件操作来映射它。

static int packet_mmap(struct file *file, struct socket *sock,
        struct vm_area_struct *vma)
{
    // ..
    for (rb = &po->rx_ring; rb <= &po->tx_ring; rb++) {
        // ..

        for (i = 0; i < rb->pg_vec_len; i++) {
            void *kaddr = rb->pg_vec[i].buffer;
            // ..

            for (pg_num = 0; pg_num < rb->pg_vec_pages; pg_num++) {
                page = pgv_to_page(kaddr);
                err = vm_insert_page(vma, start, page); // [13]
                // ..
            }
        }
    }
    // ..
}

static int insert_page(struct vm_area_struct *vma, unsigned long addr,
            struct page *page, pgprot_t prot)
{
    // ..
    retval = validate_page_before_insert(vma, page);
    if (retval)
        goto out;
    retval = -ENOMEM;
    pte = get_locked_pte(vma->vm_mm, addr, &ptl);
    if (!pte)
        goto out;
    retval = insert_page_into_pte_locked(vma, pte, addr, page, prot);
    pte_unmap_unlock(pte, ptl);
out:
    return retval;
}
static int validate_page_before_insert(struct vm_area_struct *vma,
                       struct page *page)
{
    struct folio *folio = page_folio(page);
    // ..
    if (folio_test_anon(folio) || folio_test_slab(folio) ||
        page_has_type(page))
        return -EINVAL;
    flush_dcache_folio(folio);
    return 0;
}

从代码中可以看出，通过在该文件描述符上调用 mmap，mmap 处理程序会迭代刚刚分配的 pgv 指针，并对每个指针调用 vm_insert_page [13]。 考虑到我们“控制”一个指针，这可能意味着我们可以将任意指针映射到用户空间。 这是事实，但不是全部事实。

首先，pgv_to_page 调用 virt_to_page 并通过页对齐指针（考虑到我们已经用可能不是页对齐的平板指针替换了页对齐指针）为我们带来了很大帮助，然后在其上调用 vm_insert_page 。 起初，这个想法是尝试将一些任意内存映射到用户空间（例如整个平板页、只读内存或文件，..） 但这是不允许的，因为 validate_page_before_insert 中的健全性检查验证指针不是板的一部分并且不是匿名的（例如不是文件支持的）。 我仍然认为可以用这个原语做一些有趣的事情，但是它花费了太多时间，截止日期即将到来，我必须以某种方式完成它。

查看 pgv 代码的其他部分，我在 pgv 指针的释放过程中发现了另一个有趣的原语。 在关闭与分配的 pgv 对象关联的套接字文件描述符时， free_pg_vec 迭代每个指针并在每个指针上调用 free_pages ，允许我们再次释放第二个悬空指针，但现在使用 free_pages 。 这将我们的原语升级为更强大的页面级 UAF。 并且，与前面的情况类似，指针直接在被调用的自由函数内部进行页面对齐。

然而，在 mmap 路径上花费的时间并没有完全浪费时间，即使它没有被选为最终的原语。 它已在漏洞利用中用于验证哪个 pgv 分配包含第二个悬空指针而不是合法指针，方法是将其映射到 mmap 并验证它是否成功。 如果 mmap 调用失败，则意味着 vm_insert_page 失败，我们获得两个有用的信息：第一阶段已成功，并且我们确切地知道哪个分配已损坏！

# 第二阶段：从页面UAF到物理内存r/w

一旦第一个悬空指针被交叉缓存到 pgv 对象中（我故意避免有关交叉缓存、喷射和类似技术的细节，因为这些都是众所周知的技术），我们会再次触发该错误，将第二个悬空指针存储在第一个交叉缓存悬空指针（pgv 对象）的偏移量零处。 然后，我们继续清空 skbuff_head_cache，以便将第二个悬空指针移动到每个 cpu 的 1 页空闲列表，目的是利用刚刚创建的页级 UAF。

此时，我尝试多次喷射以用一些有趣的东西回收页面指针，当我喷射一些匿名区域时，我从 gdb 转储中找到了圣杯：

0x8000000114d1c067 0x8000000114d1c077
0x8000000114d1c087 0x8000000114d1c097

看起来很熟悉吗？ 这些是 PTE！

旁注：您可能想知道我如何能够通过一些“随机”喷雾来回收订单 1 分配和订单 0 PTE 分配。 嗯，那是因为一开始我是在 2 核 qemu 环境中编写漏洞利用程序，其中 256 字节板的顺序为 0，即 skbuff_head_cache 的情况。 然而，在具有 4 个或更多核心的系统上，这些板是 1 阶的，并且漏洞利用的目标是该配置。

我立即从内存内容中发现，破坏 PTE 是最终实现物理内存读写的惊人原语。

然而，如前所述，我首先针对具有 2 个核心的 qemu 环境编写漏洞利用程序，使 skbuff_head_cache 成为 order-0 缓存而不是 order-1（pwn2own 设置可能有超过 4 个核心）。 这让我通过更多的调整来重新构建页面回收过程。 首先，第二个悬空指针从 skbuff_head_cache 排出到每 cpu 的 order-1 页空闲列表。 然后，为了将其重新分配为 0 阶分配，指针必须离开每 cpu 空闲列表页面并插入到 1 阶好友空闲列表中，从而允许将其拆分为两个 0 阶分配（因此作为 PTE 回收）。 我通过使用多个 pgv 分配来喷射和调整页面空闲列表来实现这一点。 然后，使用 mmap 通过匿名内存喷射 PTE，并且已成功从两个 0 阶分配中回收了 1 阶分配。

# 第三阶段：任意物理内存读写

一旦第二个悬空指针被回收为 PTE，我们就可以触发其上的 free_page 并用用户空间更可控的东西重新分配。 msg_msgseg 对象似乎是一个合理的选择，因为它对用户空间的内容和生命周期进行了高级别的控制（除了第一个下一个指针对于 order-0 分配将为 NULL，但这不会成为问题），甚至比 msg_msg 更好。 该目标对象的另一个关键方面是它将被分配到 kmalloc-cache 中，从而使其可以轻松地从用户空间读取和写入（考虑用户复制缓解）。

首先，消息会使用 /sys/kernel/vmcoreinfo（所有主要发行版上的非特权用户均可读取）中泄露的物理地址（转换为 PTE）进行喷射，以有效的物理地址开始。 为了找到与被覆盖的PTE对应的喷射映射，只需验证每个映射内存的内容即可。 一旦找到用户态映射，为了为任意物理读写写入新的PTE，我们可以一一接收和发送所有喷射消息（msg_msgseg对象），以避免不同对象甚至缓存的slab重新分配。 在此过程中，还可以找到正确重新分配的消息，以优化进一步的 PTE 修改并具有更高的稳定性。

# 第四阶段：uid=0

此时，距离注册截止日期还有两天，我真的很高兴能够成功，我只需要最后的、更简单的步骤。 我发了注册邮件，请了一天假，想着等注册完就回来，享受物理内存读写转化为root的工程过程。 然而，第二天、截止日期和之后都没有收到任何回复。 由于我还有很多其他工作要做，所以我刚刚完成了这个原语。

然而，从真正强大的原语到根只是稍微玩一下而已。 我的第一个想法是从 vmc​​oreinfo 泄漏（一个动态分配的对象，不属于任何静态内核地址的一部分）开始扫描内存，找到一个有效的内核地址来破坏物理地址随机化（在 Red Hat 中启用），然后从 init_struct 开始覆盖当前进程的 task_struct 或类似的东西。 不幸的是，由于其他优先级更高的待处理项目，我没有愉快地这样做。

# 关于人工智能漏洞开发和其他原语的一些注释

在这个漏洞的利用过程中，由于考虑到pwn2own的参与，它可以被认为是一个短暂的漏洞，因此我尝试使用公共前沿模型来编写完整的漏洞利用：Opus 4.8 with Max Pro plan using Claude Code。 我无论从好的方面还是坏的方面都感到惊讶。

好的部分是，它使用 red_dequeue 代码中的替代原语有效地编写了一个有效的漏洞利用程序，该原语允许重定向执行并从 sk_buff 析构函数实现 ROP 链。 它使用侧通道来进行 KASLR 泄漏，并且 pwn2own 规则考虑将其用作部分胜利，所以我想要一些更独立且具有相同错误的东西。 我使用 qemu 运行多个会话并为代理提供调试功能，但它无法从该原语中找到有效的 KASLR 泄漏。

不好的部分是，它在我在本文中解释的利用过程中无法为我提供太多帮助。 我试图用我的想法重新引导它的想法，给出一些提示和一些新的路径，但经过几个小时的后台工作，它不断地退回到侧通道泄漏和 ROP。 在某些时候，我什至怀疑我的想法是否会在模型的持续煤气灯下发挥作用，但我有一种感觉，这个错误可能比模型所说的更强大、更独立。 而且，我是对的。

也就是说，这只是我的经验，这些模型非常强大并且正在不断改进，正如漏洞开发领域的公开文章所示，很大程度上取决于您如何使用它们、有多少代币以及许多其他因素。

此外，本文的撰写并未涉及人工智能。 我发现在主要为人类编写的内容中找到人工智能模式非常无聊，我更喜欢看到一种不完美但个人和人类可识别的写作风格。 如果您看到一些错误，只需像以前一样在 X 上 ping 我即可。

# 结论

我没有过多谈论喷涂细节、稳定性和可靠性改进，即使它们大约占工作的 60-70%。 我想更多地关注利用策略和错误根本原因的一般思想。 我没有计划很快发布该漏洞，因为该漏洞还很新鲜，而且在撰写本文时也没有分配 CVE。

关于pwn2own注册失败的事情，我不是唯一有这种经历的人，我不怪该组织。 这是不幸的，考虑到几年前我也发布了关于 Hacktive Security 的一次失败尝试，我真的很高兴能参与其中。 然而，人工智能已经改变了很多防御/进攻的猫鼠游戏，需要一段时间（也许几年）才能找到新的平衡和更加平静的水域。 也许，也许不是。
